text: The surgeon gently probes the fistula tract, carefully following the pathological path, with the aid of a speculum inserted into the anal canal.
The surgeon uses an umbrella handle-shaped probe to align Morgagni's crypt with the fistula tract, ensuring precise alignment for further surgical intervention.

The surgeon palpates to identify the intersphincteric groove and opens it using diathermia. The intersphincteric space is developed, revealing the intersphincteric fistula tract. The fistula tract is encircled and ligated with simple ligations, although transfixion sutures are noted to be preferable.
The surgeon divides the fistula tract, separating it from surrounding tissue within the intersphincteric space.

The surgeon identifies the internal opening of the fistula and performs a core-out fistulectomy up to the external anal sphincter. The internal opening is enlarged, and the remaining fistula is removed through it. The outlines of the flap are marked, and a partial thickness flap is raised using a harmonic scalpel, consisting of mucosa, submucosa, and superficial circular muscular fibers.
The surgeon creates an adequate flap and closes the internal opening with interrupted vicryl sutures. The flap is then secured using two barbed sutures.
The surgeon completes the closure of the fistula, ensuring the closure is adequate and secure.
